title: Digestive biscuits
subtitle: Wholemeal, slightly oaty, properly nutty
excerpt: Homemade digestives with coarse wholemeal flour and oats: slightly crumbly at the edge, with a tender centre and the faintly sweet, nutty quality that makes them work as well with cheese as with chocolate.
sourceNotes: Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: basic wholemeal biscuit formula. The digestive biscuit was popularised as a domestic bake in the 1880s; early recipes used bicarbonate of soda (the 'digestive' claim referred to the raising agent, not any nutritional benefit). Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the rubbing-in method for plain biscuits.

content.text: A homemade digestive is noticeably different from the commercial version: coarser in texture, more pronouncedly wholemeal, and with a slightly sandy edge where the commercial one is uniformly crisp. That roughness is the point. The oats add their own flavour; the bicarbonate of soda gives a very faint alkaline background note that keeps the biscuit from being merely sweet.

, more fat than liquid, which keeps gluten development low and gives the biscuit its crumble. Work quickly and chill the dough before rolling: warm fat makes the dough sticky and the baked biscuit soft rather than crisp.

attrs.prepNote: blitzed briefly to a coarse powder

content.text: Combine the {{wholemeal-flour}}, blitzed {{rolled-oats}}, {{demerara-sugar}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} in a large bowl. Add the cold {{unsalted-butter}}. Rub it in with your fingertips until the mix looks like damp sand with no visible lumps of butter.

Add the {{whole-milk}} one tablespoon at a time, stirring with a knife after each addition, until the dough just comes together. It should not be sticky. Press it into a disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the dough between two sheets of baking paper to about 4 mm thick. Cut rounds with a 7 cm cutter and lift onto the trays. Gather the scraps, re-roll once, and cut again. Prick each biscuit several times with a fork to prevent it puffing up in the oven.

Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until the biscuits are an even pale gold. They will still feel slightly soft in the centre when you take them out; they firm up as they cool on the tray. Slide onto a rack after 5 minutes. Store in a tin once cold.
